coredata.dc:title: Iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles exhibiting zymolyase-like lytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Menglin
Li, Na
Lv, Shanshan
coredata.dc:description: Iron oxide (Fe3O4) magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were found to exhibit an intrinsic yeast lytic activity with enzyme kinetics similar to that of natural zymolyase. The Fe3O4 MNPs were synthesized by a co-precipitation method and were then employed to catalyze the disruption of yeast cells. The Fe3O4 MNPs accelerated the biocatalytic reaction to completion within 10 min. Compared to natural zymolyase, the newly discovered biocatalytic activity of Fe3O4 was outstanding and remained stable against temperature and pH changes. More importantly, Fe3O4 MNPs with excellent activity could be successfully synthesized using a high-gravity co-precipitation method promising for large scale production in industrial applications. Considering its cost-effectiveness and ease of preparation, Fe3O4 was expected to show great potential for the digestion of yeast cell walls through serving as nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes or nanozymes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zymolyase
Yeast cells
Iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles
Nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes
Nanozymes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: β-1,3-glucanase- and alkaline protease-like activity of Fe3O4
Possible large scale production of Fe3O4
Determination of lytic actions of Fe3O4 MNPs on yeast cells (zymolyase-like activity)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis process and bio-catalytic reaction of the Fe3O4 MNPs.
Chemical composition of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs. a) XRD patterns, b) FTIR spectroscopy, c) Fe 2p and d) O1s XPS spectra, confirming Fe3O4.
Morphology of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs. Representative ab) SEM and cd) HRTEM images with scale bars of 100 nm and 10 nm, respectively.

Zymolyase-like activity of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs. a) Time course of lysis of yeast cells. b) The double reciprocal plot of Lineweaver-Burk (velocity as a function of the initial concentration) of the as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs for yeast cells with different initial concentrations. The plot could be well fitted with a linear equation,
y = 1.69 x + 3.42 ×
10
- 5
. c) The effect of temperature on lytic activity at a fixed pH of 7.5. d) The effect of pH on lytic activity at a fixed temperature of 25 °C. Error bars represent standard deviation of at least three measurements.

head.ce:title: Iron oxide magnetic nanoparticles exhibiting zymolyase-like lytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composite Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, 15 BeisanhuanDong Road, Chaoyang District, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composite Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composite Materials, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, 15 BeisanhuanDong Road, Chaoyang District, Beijing 100029, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Iron oxide (Fe3O4) magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were found to exhibit an intrinsic yeast lytic activity with enzyme kinetics similar to that of natural zymolyase. The Fe3O4 MNPs were synthesized by a co-precipitation method and were then employed to catalyze the disruption of yeast cells. The Fe3O4 MNPs accelerated the biocatalytic reaction to completion within 10 min. Compared to natural zymolyase, the newly discovered biocatalytic activity of Fe3O4 was outstanding and remained stable against temperature and pH changes. More importantly, Fe3O4 MNPs with excellent activity could be successfully synthesized using a high-gravity co-precipitation method promising for large scale production in industrial applications. Considering its cost-effectiveness and ease of preparation, Fe3O4 was expected to show great potential for the digestion of yeast cell walls through serving as nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes or nanozymes.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials which possess intrinsic enzyme-like activity are more commonly referred to as nanomaterial-based artificial enzymes or “nanozymes” [1–3]. Nanozymes have successfully served as and have been recognized as a viable alternative to natural enzymes for catalysis in a wide range of applications, including biosensing, bioimaging, biomedicine and beyond [2–4]. The nanozyme activities range from peroxidase, haloperoxidase, NADH peroxidase, oxidase, glucose oxidase and sulfite oxidase, superoxide dismutase (SOD) to catalase. Diverse nanozymes such as fullerene derivatives, metal-carbon compound, metal (platinum (Pt), palladium (Pd) and gold (Au)) nanoparticles, metal oxide, metal sulfide, rare earth nanoparticles and ferromagnetic nanoparticles have been reported [5–12]. Iron oxide Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) are the first reported nanomaterials with intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, with catalytic behavior similar to horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [13]. Fe3O4 MNPs were also found to have an intrinsic catalase-like activity [14]. Based on the peroxidase- and catalase-like activities, a variety of applications of Fe3O4 MNPs have been developed. For example, Fe3O4 MNPs were used in colorimetric analysis for sensitive detection of H2O2 and glucose [15]. Beside of the colorimetric sensor, Fe3O4 MNPs were used in fabrication of electrochemical sensors for H2O2 detection [16]. In addition, Fe3O4 MNPs were used in design of inexpensive immunoassays, especially for simple and rapid diagnosis of Ebola virus [17–19]. Moreover, Fe3O4 MNPs were used in bioimaging of tumor tissues and treatment of cancer cells [20–22]. Furthermore, Fe3O4 MNPs were also used for degradation removal of organic pollutant from wastewater [23,24].

Although much progress has been made to explore the Fe3O4 MNPs for various biocatalytic activities and corresponding applications, few studies reported lytic activity. Zymolyase is an enzyme mixture accumulated in Arthrobacter luteus culture fluid with very strong lytic activity on viable yeast cells. The cytoplasm of (Saccharomyces cerevisiae, S. cerevisiae) yeast cell is a rich source of proteins and polysaccharides which are valuable in biotechnology and pharmaceutical industries. Existence of relatively thick and rigid cell walls in S. cerevisiae yeast cells makes complete disruption of the cell walls necessary to recover intracellular bio-products [25]. Zymolyase could mainly be separated into two protein fractions, which are β-l,3-glucanase and alkaline protease, respectively. Neither fraction alone could lyse yeast cells, but a mixture of the two fractions together showed high lytic activity for efficient breakage of yeast cells by digesting cell walls [26]. Although efficient, zymolyase bears some intrinsic disadvantages of natural enzymes such as time-consuming and high-cost preparation and purification, low operational stability and specific storage conditions, which limit their industrial applications on the large scale [27]. In this aspect, Fe3O4 nanozymes exhibit superiority over natural enzymes in terms of low synthesis cost, ease of bulk production, reusability, high stability and tolerance to harsh operational and storage condition.

In this study, we found that Fe3O4 MNPs exhibited an intrinsic zymolyase-like lytic activity, and we made use of the newly discovered properties of the Fe3O4 MNPs to digest cell walls of yeast (Fig. 1 ). First, the Fe3O4 MNPs were synthesized using a co-precipitation method. Second, characteristics such as composition, morphology and structure of the Fe3O4 MNPs were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), and tuned by controlling reaction parameters of synthesis strategies including a high-gravity co-precipitation method. Third, disruption of S. cerevisiae yeast cells using the Fe3O4 MNPs as bio-catalysts was investigated by UV/Vis spectroscopy. The effects of temperature and pH on cell disruption were examined. Finally, the zymolyase-mimic catalytic activity of the Fe3O4 MNPs was investigated in details with focus on the reaction kinetics. The Fe3O4 MNPs show great potential of developing into efficient, robust, and low-cost alternative materials that could replace natural zymolyase, and providing a valuable alternative method that is favored for industrial-scale cell disruption.

Crystalline structures of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs were confirmed with powder XRD measurements (Fig. 2 a). The characteristic diffraction peaks with 2θ values of 30.1, 35.4, 37.1, 43.1, 53.4, 57.0, and 62.6° could be devoted to (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 2 2), (5 1 1) and (4 4 0) crystal planes of Fe3O4 (Powder Diffraction File #85-1436), respectively [15,27–29]. FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2b) showed a band at 605 cm−1, which could be attributed to the Fe-O-Fe stretching and bending modes of Fe3O4 [27], and a band at 3383 cm−1 to the hydroxyl group (–OH) of adsorbed water [30]. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs showed a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 100.6 ± 0.3 m2·g−1 (Fig. S1). XPS spectra showed peaks at 709.7 eV and 723.6 eV, implying that the oxidation state of Fe could be assigned to Fe 2p, while that at 529.7 eV to O 1s [31], and thereby verified the composition of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs (Fig. 2cd). The Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) also indicated presence of Fe and O in the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs (Fig. S2a) [27]. The morphology and structure of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs was further investigated with both SEM and HRTEM analysis (Fig. 3 ). From the SEM images, the average diameter of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs was estimated to be ~17.7 nm (Fig. S2b). From the HRTEM images, the interplanar spacing was measured to be ~0.237 nm, which could be assigned to the (3 1 1) crystallographic orientation of cubic Fe3O4 [32]. These observations demonstrated successful synthesis of Fe3O4 MNPs.

To investigate the zymolyase-like activity of the as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs, the catalytic disruption of yeast cells was tested [26,33]. The efficiency of cell disruption was evaluated by measurement of UV/Vis spectroscopy (Fig. S3a), and was expressed as percentage decrease in the number of intact yeast cells during the reaction time (Eq. (1)) [27]. Fig. 4 a and Fig. S3b showed time course of the lytic reaction at 25 °C and pH 7.5. The as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs exhibited a lytic activity; at an initial concentration of ~7000 cfu·mL−1, almost complete lysis of the yeast cells could be achieved within ~10 min by the Fe3O4 MNPs, while zymolyase has been reported to take more than 120 min [27]. Furthermore, the catalytic lysis reaction followed typical Michaelis-Menten (steady-state) kinetics, as with many of natural enzymes (Eq. (2)). Within a range of yeast cell concentrations from 5000 to 11000 cfu·mL−1, a Lineweaver-Burk plot (
1 / v
as a linear function of
1 / [ S ]
, Fig. 4b) was drawn to determine the corresponding apparent kinetic parameters including
K m

K
cat
(the turnover number) and

K m
(Eqs. (3)–(6)) [27]. The plot was linear, and the
K m
value was calculated to be 4.94 × 104 cfu·mL−1 (assuming one yeast cell per colony forming unit (cfu) in the present study), illustrating high affinity between Fe3O4 and yeast cells. The
K
cat
/
K m
value was 15.2 L·mol−1·h−1, the
K
cat
value was 7.53 × 105 cfu·mol−1·h−1, confirming activity of the synthesized Fe3O4 MNPs. Based on these kinetic parameters, it would be reasonable to conclude that the Fe3O4 MNPs mimic zymolyase activity.

t

As natural enzymes, the catalytic activity of zymolyase for lysis of yeast cells is sensitive to pH and temperature; the optimum pH is 7.5 and the optimum temperature is 35 °C [26,27]. For natural zymolyase, a previous study reported that almost no lytic activity could be detected at pH ≤ 5 [27]; and the enzymatic activity decreased significantly as the temperature increased from 30 to 60 °C (Fig. S3c). The pH- and temperature-dependent response of the Fe3O4 MNPs was investigated at a yeast cell concentration of ~7000 cfu·mL−1 (Fig. 4cd). Different from natural zymolyase displaying bell-shaped curves, the as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs were highly stable in the pH range of 5–10 and the temperature range of 25–60 °C. It is of note that iron ions might be leached from the Fe3O4 MNPs in the reaction mixtures [13,34]. Thus, the catalytic activity of the iron ions that leached in pH 5–10 buffer solutions was further tested. Comparing to the Fe3O4 MNPs, no significant catalytic activity of the leached iron ions was observed, ensuring that the catalytic activity originated from Fe3O4. In addition, the Fe3O4 MNPs could be magnetically separated, recovered, and reused with a relatively high efficiency; in the second cycle after 20 h storage, the Fe3O4 MNPs exhibited ~80% of the activity in the first cycle (Fig. S3d). These results demonstrated wider range of temperature and pH tolerance as well as improved recyclability of the as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs than that of natural zymolyase [26,27], implying their attractive potential to be included for practical application in industrial processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: β-1,3-glucanase- and alkaline protease-like activity of Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: Since zymolyase could be separated into β-l,3-glucanase and alkaline protease [26,27], in order to further explore generation of the zymolyase-like activity of the as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs, β-1,3-glucanase- and alkaline protease-like activities were investigated using laminarin [35] and casein [36] as substrates, respectively. β-1,3-glucanase can catalyze endohydrolysis of laminarin with β-1,3-linkages into products with laminaripentaose as the minimum unit; the products can be determined by the Somogyi method using glucose as standard. Meanwhile, protease can catalyze hydrolysis of proteins into small peptides and amino acids, and alkaline protease represents protease which is active in neutral and alkaline pH; the products can be determined by the Folin’s reagent using tyrosine as standard. However, neither β-1,3-glucanase- nor alkaline protease-like activity was observed for the as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs (Fig. S4). These observations suggested that the Fe3O4 MNPs lyse yeast cells through a different mechanism from that of zymolyase. The unique nature of the lytic activity of the Fe3O4 MNPs deserves a more detailed investigation in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible large scale production of Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: Conventional co-precipitation synthesis of Fe3O4 MNPs is often carried out in stirred tank reactors, in which it will be time-consuming and difficult to control the quality (such as the morphology and size distribution) of the product on large scale. The high-gravity technology is a well-established chemical engineering method prestigious for easy scale-up for industrialization. The principle is to generate a high-gravity environment via a large centrifugal force, which would spread/split reactant solutions into tiny droplets as the solutions flow through a stainless wire mesh packing, leading to micro-mixing which can intensify mass and heat transfer effectively. In our previous studies, we employed a high-gravity rotating packed bed (RPB) reactor for successful large scale production of hydroxyapatite-based NPs [37,38]. Therefore, we prepared Fe3O4 MNPs via a modified high-gravity co-precipitation method [28,39] without adding any surfactants, and explored possibility of utilizing the resultant Fe3O4 MNPs for digesting yeast cells (Fig. S5). Change in the rotating rates (corresponding to high-gravity levels) did not significantly affect the crystalline structures, chemical composition and particle size. All Fe3O4 MNPs synthesized under different high-gravity levels exhibited zymolyase-like activity.

Fe3O4 MNPs were prepared using a co-precipitation method and their intrinsic zymolyase-like activity was explored. The as-prepared Fe3O4 MNPs were successfully used for the disruption of S. cerevisiae cells. The reaction data of lysis of yeast cells were fit to a pseudo-first order kinetic expression. In addition, the Fe3O4 nanozymes could be fabricated in a feasible and low-cost high-gravity co-precipitation process for commercial massive production. It is important to note that the zymolyase-like activity of the Fe3O4 MNPs could not be explained by the β-1,3-glucanase and alkaline protease activity in zymolyase. The intrinsic nature of the zymolyase-like activity of the Fe3O4 MNPs has not yet been revealed for now; further studies are needed to shed more insight into the mechanism of cell disruption by the Fe3O4 MNPs. Nevertheless, because of the simple and cost-effective preparation, robustness, stability in harsh conditions, and easy to scale up, the Fe3O4 MNPs show great potential applications in providing a new and effective method for cell disruption, not only in laboratory conditions, but also on industrial scale. The newly discovered zymolyase-like activity of the Fe3O4 MNPs will also facilitate the study of nanomaterials as enzyme mimics or nanozymes.

Zymolyase from Arthrobacter luteus was purchased from NACALAI TESQUE, INC. (Japan). NaOH (98%), FeCl2·4H2O (98%) and FeCl3·6H2O (99%) were purchased from Macklin. Analytical reagent grade absolute ethanol and all other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co. Ltd., and used without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified by a Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA) unless otherwise stated.

The Fe3O4 MNPs were prepared via a previously reported co-precipitation method [28]. Solutions of FeCl3 and FeCl2 were mixed (at the Fe2+: Fe3+ molar ratio of 1:2); pH of the above mixture was adjusted to 10 with NaOH solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min at 40 °C in a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere. The black Fe3O4 MNPs formed under the idealized stoichiometric equation (Eq. (7)) were separated by magnet, washed thoroughly with ethanol, and dried in the oven at 50 °C.
(7)
2 F e C
l 3
+ F e C
l 2
+ 8 N a O H
→
N 2
F
e 3
O 4
↓ + 8 N a C l + 4
H 2
O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of lytic actions of Fe3O4 MNPs on yeast cells (zymolyase-like activity)
ce:section.ce:para: The yeast cell lysis activity was assayed as described previously [27]. S. cerevisiae YS58 yeast cells were cultivated in medium (glucose 2.0%, peptone 2.0%, pH 5.5) at 30 °C for 2 days, harvested by centrifugation, and washed with cold water. Yeast cell suspensions was prepared by re-dispersing the yeast cells in 1/15 M phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) with gentle stirring (in order to prevent any damage of the yeast cells) until homogeneous pre-mixtures were obtained. A mixture of 1.8 mg of Fe3O4 MNPs and 200 μL of yeast cell suspension was incubated at 25 °C for 2 h with gentle shaking in wort (10° plato). During the lytic reactions, the turbidity (optical density (OD) at 620 nm) of the reaction mixture was determined by a UV/Vis spectrophotometer, and the number of intact yeast cells was examined under the microscope. As blank experiments, phosphate buffer was used instead of Fe3O4 MNPs. For comparison purposes, zymolyase (10 mg·mL−1, 10 μL) was used instead of Fe3O4 MNPs. The appropriate initial concentrations of yeast cell was set within the OD620nm range of [0.5, 0.7] according to the manufacturer protocol.

Temperature. To examine the influence of temperature on the lytic activity of Fe3O4 MNPs, reactions incubated at different temperature from 30 °C to 60 °C were investigated under conditions identical to those used for 25 °C.
pH. To examine the influence of pH on the lytic activity of Fe3O4 MNPs, phosphate buffer from pH 4 to 10 were investigated under conditions identical to those used for pH 7.5.

Leaching of iron ions. To investigate the leaching of iron ions from Fe3O4 MNPs, Fe3O4 MNPs were incubated in the reaction buffer solutions with different pH values. Then Fe3O4 MNPs were removed by an external magnetic field. The catalytic activity of the leaching solutions was investigated under conditions identical to those used for pH 7.5 at 25 °C.

Recyclability test. After the first cycle, the Fe3O4 MNPs were collected using a magnet, washed with PBS buffers and H2O, respectively. In running the second cycle, the recovered Fe3O4 MNPs was re-added into fresh yeast cell suspension. The activity of Fe3O4 MNPs was normalized to that in the first cycle, which was attributed a relative activity of 100%.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Prof. Yan Zhang (Tianjin University) for helpful suggestion, Dr. Junfeng Liu (Beijing University of Chemical Technology) for kind supply of yeast cells, Dr. Baochang Sun (Beijing University of Chemical Technology) for kind assistance on high gravity experiments, Qinfu Lu and Zihua Dong (Beijing University of Chemical Technology) for kind assistance on the recyclability test.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers: 21972009 and 31400813], the National Key Research Program of China [grant number: 2016YFA0201700/2016YFA0201701], and the Foundation of Beijing University of Chemical Technology [grant number: XK180301].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.125000.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
